title: DSDS States Document Block
description: Documents all interactive states a component can enter: hover, focus, active, disabled, loading, selected, error, and so on. Each item describes a single state: what triggers it, how appearance and behavior change, which design tokens are overridden, and how the state looks.

stateEntry.description: A single interactive state of a component. States are transient or persistent visual or behavioral changes triggered by user interaction, system events, or data conditions.

description.description: What triggers this state, how appearance and behavior change, and any constraints or side effects.

tokens.description: Design token overrides applied when this state is active, as the shared token-override map: keys are token-purpose names, values are documented token identifiers. List only tokens that change from the default state. Each state carries its own overrides here; on components, baseline (default-state) values live on the design-specifications block (patterns have no such block — their anatomy carries the baseline).

rationale.description: Why this state exists — the user need or design rationale it addresses.

examples.description: Examples showing the component in this state.

states.description: Documents all interactive states of a component or pattern. Each item describes a single state: what triggers it, how appearance and behavior change, which tokens are overridden, and how it looks. List states in a meaningful order. A typical order is default first, then hover, focus, active, followed by disabled, loading, and other special states. Ownership rule: a state is a runtime condition entered through interaction or system events (hover, focus, disabled, loading); a configuration the consumer chooses up front is a variant — document it in the variants block, not here.

kind.description: Identifies this document block as a states spec.

items.description: The interactive states. Each item documents a single state with its trigger, visual changes, token overrides, and optional examples. Ordering is significant, so tools SHOULD display states in this order.
